title: Slow-cooker beef stroganoff
subtitle: Beef chuck, mushrooms, and soured cream
excerpt: Strips of beef chuck braised in beef stock with chestnut mushrooms, Dijon mustard, and smoked paprika for six hours, then finished with soured cream. The slow cooker tenderises the chuck completely — the result is a richer dish than the pan-fried version. Serve with rice or egg noodles.
sourceNotes: Beef stroganoff originated in Russian aristocratic cooking in the 19th century and spread globally through the 20th century. The British version became a dinner party favourite in the 1970s using rump or fillet. The slow cooker adaptation uses beef chuck instead — a cheaper, more marbled cut that suits the long braise. The soured cream is always stirred in at the end, never cooked from the start.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook the beef through the slow-cooker stage and refrigerate. Reheat gently and stir in the soured cream just before serving.

content.text: The soured cream goes in at the end — stir it through once the slow cooker is off and the dish has stopped bubbling. Adding it while the pot is still on high heat will cause it to split into grainy curds rather than coating the beef in a smooth, creamy sauce.

Add the {{beef-chuck}}, {{onion}}, {{garlic}}, {{mushrooms-chestnut}}, {{dijon-mustard}}, {{worcestershire-sauce}}, {{smoked-paprika}}, {{tomato-puree}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}} to the slow cooker. Pour in the {{stock-beef}} and stir everything to combine. Cook on low for 6 to 8 hours, until the beef is completely tender and pulling apart. Switch the slow cooker off. Wait 5 minutes for the liquid to stop simmering, then stir in the {{soured-cream}} until smooth. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve immediately over rice or egg noodles.

items.symptom: Soured cream has split into grainy bits
items.cause: Added while the pot was still bubbling
items.fix: Switch the slow cooker off and wait 5 minutes before stirring in the soured cream. The residual heat is enough to warm it through without causing it to split
Fry the mushrooms in butter over high heat until browned before adding them to the slow cooker. Raw mushrooms in a slow cooker release a lot of liquid

items.cause: Chuck is thick and needs longer at low temperature
items.fix: Add another hour on low or switch to high for the final hour. Chuck will only get more tender with time

content.text: Stroganoff was a 1970s dinner party dish that faded from fashion and came back as a weeknight option. The slow cooker version is simpler than the original — no timing required, no risk of overcooking. The beef chuck is cheaper and more forgiving than fillet or rump. Serve over rice with a green salad alongside.
